_note: Launch-day X thread for @przm_ai. Edit before --send. Each entry must be ≤280 chars (the script validates). Posts chain as replies in order.
tweets:
I built a benchmark for something nobody is measuring: how multi-agent AI systems collapse to confidently-wrong answers when one agent gets seeded with a fake-but-confident position.

On the sealed holdout: AutoGen 0 of 6 collapses, baseline 5 of 6.

przm.sh

thread ↓

The setup is simple. N agents debate a question. One is given a wrong answer plus a polished rationale. The rest start correct.

We measure how many of the correct ones end up agreeing with the wrong one. And how much of that depends on the framework, not the model.

gpt-4o-mini, 30 scenarios, 3 agents × 3 rounds.

• Hand-rolled synchronous: 90% collapse
• Sequential-reveal baseline: 83% collapse
• AutoGen RoundRobin: 20% collapse

Sealed 6-fixture holdout: baseline 83%, sequential 67%, AutoGen 0%.

Same model. Different framework.

Why the sequential baseline matters: AutoGen uses sequential reveal (agent N sees what 0..N-1 said this round). Without a sequential baseline, we'd be conflating orchestration with reveal protocol.

The gap holds after controlling for it.

Methodology is deterministic. No LLM in the grading loop.

Every result is an Ed25519-signed receipt: adapter version, LLM, fixture SHA, per-round transcripts. Same fixture + same code → byte-identical signature. Verify in your browser at /verify.

Open source.

przm.sh
